schools.summary: Gill: 'the tongues of men' are all the languages men anywhere speak; Lightfoot discusses 'the tongues of angels' (the highest conceivable speech); Paul declaring that though he speak with the tongues of men and of angels, without charity (love) he is but sounding brass or a tinkling cymbal — the supremacy of love over the most exalted gift of utterance (the gift of tongues, even raised to the highest conceivable pitch, the speech of angels, is without love mere empty noise, a hollow clanging that profits nothing), the rebuke to the Corinthians who prized tongues above all (without love it is nothing).

themes: the tongues of men and of angels
have not charity
as sounding brass, or a tinkling cymbal
the supremacy of love over tongues

schools.summary: Gill: the gift of prophecy (foretelling, as Balaam, who foretold of the Messiah, and Caiaphas, who prophesied of Christ's death, both without love; or expounding, or preaching), the highest knowledge, and the faith of miracles; Paul declaring that though he have prophecy, all mysteries, all knowledge, and all faith to remove mountains (Matthew 17:20), without charity he is nothing — the supremacy of love over the gifts of revelation and power (even the deepest understanding and the mountain-moving faith, without love, leave a man nothing, not merely his gifts but himself worthless before God), the gifts possessable without grace (as Balaam, Caiaphas, Judas).

themes: the gift of prophecy... all mysteries, and all knowledge
all faith, so that I could remove mountains
have not charity, I am nothing
love above prophecy, knowledge, and faith

schools.summary: Gill: the Jews gave instances of those who distributed all their goods to the poor; Paul speaks even of giving the body to be burned (martyrdom by fire) — yet without love these profit nothing; Paul declaring that though he give all his goods to the poor and his body to be burned, without charity it profits him nothing — the supremacy of love over the costliest deeds (even the most extreme outward works, giving away all in alms and suffering martyrdom, if without love, done from vainglory or any motive but love, profit the doer nothing before God, though they may benefit others), love above almsgiving and martyrdom.

themes: though I bestow all my goods to feed the poor
though I give my body to be burned
have not charity, it profiteth me nothing
love above almsgiving and martyrdom

schools.summary: Chrysostom: having shown that faith, knowledge, prophecy, tongues, gifts, a perfect life, and martyrdom, if love be absent, are no advantage, Paul now makes an outline of love's matchless beauty, adorning its image with the parts of virtue as with colours and putting together all its members with exactness, bidding us examine each with care to know the treasure in it; Paul beginning the portrait of love — it suffers long (is patient under wrong, slow to anger), is kind (benevolent, gentle), envies not (grieves not at others' good), vaunts not itself (boasts not, is not ostentatious), is not puffed up (not proud or conceited) — the character of love in its active graces and its freedom from the vices.

themes: charity suffereth long, and is kind
charity envieth not
vaunteth not itself, is not puffed up
the portrait of love's character

schools.summary: Gill: love does not behave itself unseemly — uses no unbecoming words or indecent actions, is careful no filthy or corrupt communication proceed from the mouth, nor ridiculous gestures that grieve the saints; Paul continuing the portrait — love behaves not unseemly (is courteous, decorous), seeks not her own (is unselfish, seeking others' good not her own interest), is not easily provoked (even-tempered, not stirred to anger or resentment), thinks no evil (is guileless, harbouring no suspicion or grudge, devising no harm, imputing not evil motives) — the further graces of love.

themes: doth not behave itself unseemly
seeketh not her own
is not easily provoked
thinketh no evil

schools.summary: Gill: love rejoices not in iniquity — neither in its own nor in others'; but mourns over its own sins, the corruption of its heart, and its secret sins, and is grieved at the profaneness of the world and the sins of professors; nor does it rejoice in any injustice or injury done to any, even an enemy; Paul declaring that love rejoices not in iniquity but rejoices in the truth — love's moral discernment (taking no pleasure in sin, whether its own, others', or any wrong done to any, but grieving over it, and instead rejoicing in the truth, the gospel, righteousness, and the good and right).

themes: rejoiceth not in iniquity
but rejoiceth in the truth
love's moral discernment
grieving over sin, delighting in righteousness

schools.summary: Gill: love 'beareth all things' — the burdens of fellow Christians (fulfilling the law of Christ), the infirmities of the weak, the reproaches and persecutions of the world; or 'covereth all things,' even a multitude of sins (1 Peter 4:8), by forgiving freely; Paul completing the portrait — love bears all things, believes all things (the best, not suspicious or censorious), hopes all things (for good, even where it doubts, hoping the best for the erring), endures all things (trials, persecutions, wrongs, to the end) — the matchless patience and charity of love, its four-fold 'all things' showing its charity without limit.

themes: beareth all things, believeth all things
hopeth all things, endureth all things
love's four-fold endurance
the matchless charity of love

schools.summary: Chrysostom: Paul points out love's worth from a fresh head — persuading those accounted inferior that it is in their power to have the chief of all signs (love), and that they will be no worse off than the possessors of the gifts but much better; Paul declaring that charity never fails, but prophecies shall fail, tongues shall cease, and knowledge (the gift) shall vanish away — the supremacy of love in its permanence (love abiding forever, while the spiritual gifts, being for the present imperfect state, shall cease when their purpose is fulfilled, v.10), so that love outlasts the very gifts the Corinthians prized.

themes: charity never faileth
prophecies... shall fail; tongues... shall cease
knowledge... shall vanish away
love's permanence against the gifts' cessation

schools.summary: Gill: this means not that the Scriptures (the rule of knowledge) are imperfect, so as to need unwritten traditions or new revelations (they contain a complete system, to which nothing is added and from which nothing taken), nor that only some saints know, but that the saints' knowledge in this life is partial and imperfect — they know in part and prophesy (preach, expound) in part; Paul declaring that we know in part and prophesy in part — the imperfection of present spiritual attainment (the believer's knowledge and the prophet's gift, however real, partial and fragmentary in this life, mere portions of the full understanding reserved for the world to come, vv.10, 12).

themes: we know in part
we prophesy in part
the imperfection of present attainment
portions of the full to come

schools.summary: Gill: 'that which is perfect' is the perfect knowledge of God, of Christ, and of the mysteries of the kingdom of heaven, which shall take place not in this life but in the life to come (the Jews placing it at the resurrection, on the reunion of soul and body); Paul declaring that when that which is perfect is come, the partial shall be done away — the principle of the perfect displacing the imperfect (when the perfect state arrives, the partial knowledge and prophecy vanish, as the dawn displaces the candle).

debates.question: What is 'that which is perfect' that displaces the partial gifts?

sides.position: The eternal/heavenly state — the beatific vision at Christ's coming or the resurrection (when we see 'face to face' and 'know even as we are known,' v.12, pointing beyond the canon)
The completion of the canon of Scripture — the partial revelatory gifts (prophecy, tongues, word of knowledge) ceasing when the full revelation was given

themes: when that which is perfect is come
that which is in part shall be done away
the perfect displacing the partial
the debate on 'the perfect'

schools.summary: Gill: Paul illustrates the present gifts (as tongues, an extraordinary gift many were fond of), which in their highest exercise were but like the lisping of a child compared to what shall be known and expressed when the saints are perfect men in heaven; Paul illustrating the passing of the partial by the growth from child to man — as a child he spoke, understood, and thought as a child, but becoming a man put away childish things — the figure of maturity displacing immaturity (the present gifts and knowledge, however real, to the coming perfection as a child's speech and thought to a grown man's, to be left behind when the believer reaches maturity in glory).

themes: when I was a child, I spake as a child
when I became a man, I put away childish things
maturity displacing immaturity
the present gifts left behind in glory

schools.summary: Gill: now, in this life, the enlightened see God, the perfections and glory of his nature, and the glory of Christ, but through a glass, darkly (as in a mirror, or an enigma/riddle); then they shall see face to face, in full and immediate vision; Paul contrasting the present dim sight (through a glass, darkly) with the future direct vision (face to face), and the present partial knowledge with the future knowledge 'even as also I am known' — the great hope of the beatific vision (the believer's present knowledge of God dim and indirect, as a reflection in a clouded mirror, but in glory clear, immediate, and full, knowing God as fully, in proportion, as God now knows him).

themes: now we see through a glass, darkly
but then face to face
then shall I know even as also I am known
the hope of the beatific vision

schools.summary: Gill: faith here is not the faith of miracles (which does not abide), nor a mere historical faith, but the faith peculiar to God's elect, a fruit of electing grace, which abides; and so hope and charity — the principal graces of the Spirit; Paul declaring that now abide faith, hope, and charity, these three, but the greatest is charity — the crowning conclusion (against the transient gifts, vv.8, which cease, stand the three abiding graces; and of these the greatest is love, for it is the fulfilling of the law, Romans 13:10, the bond of perfectness, Colossians 3:14, and the grace that abides even in heaven when faith is turned to sight and hope to fruition, the more excellent way, 12:31).

themes: now abideth faith, hope, charity, these three
the greatest of these is charity
the three abiding graces
love abides even in heaven
